questions.prompt: What is the fundamental behavioral difference between a struct and a class in Swift?

options.text: A struct is a value type — assigning or passing it copies it, so each holder has an independent instance; a class is a reference type — assigning or passing it shares a reference to one instance, so mutations are visible to every holder.
A struct is stored on the heap and a class on the stack; the copy behavior is identical between them.
A struct can have methods but a class cannot; otherwise they behave the same.
A class copies on assignment while a struct shares a reference — structs are the reference type in Swift.

questions.explanation: The chapter's core distinction: value type = copy on assign/pass (independent), reference type = share (mutations visible to all). (b) is wrong on both storage and behavior — and storage location is not the defining difference. (c) is false; both structs and classes have methods. (d) exactly reverses which is the value and which is the reference type.

questions.prompt: Swift's Array and String are value types, yet assigning a large array to a new variable is cheap. Why?

options.text: Copy-on-write: the underlying buffer is shared between the two variables until one of them mutates it, and only then is the buffer actually copied — giving value semantics (nobody sees your changes) with reference-level cost for reads and non-mutating assignments.
Because Array and String are secretly reference types, so no copy ever happens even on mutation.
The compiler skips the copy entirely and both variables permanently share storage, so mutating one does change the other.
Swift caches every array globally, so assignments just return an index into the cache.

questions.explanation: Copy-on-write is the chapter's named technique: shared buffer until mutation, copy only on write — value semantics at reference-level read cost. (b) is false; they are value types, and mutation does trigger a copy. (c) describes reference sharing, which would break value semantics — the opposite of what COW preserves. (d) fabricates a global cache mechanism.

questions.prompt: Why does the marked line fail to compile?

options.text: increment() is a mutating method, and a mutating method changes the value itself; calling it on a let constant would require replacing the constant's value, which is not allowed — the fix is declaring c with var.
private(set) makes count entirely unwritable, so no method can ever change it, even a mutating one.
Structs cannot have methods that modify their properties at all; you must use a class.
The initializer is missing, so Counter() is invalid.

questions.explanation: The chapter explains that a mutating method conceptually replaces the value, so it can't be called on a let (the constant is the value). Declaring c as var fixes it. (b) misreads private(set) — it restricts external writes but the type's own mutating methods can still change count. (c) is false; structs absolutely have mutating methods. (d) is false; the memberwise/default init makes Counter() valid.

questions.prompt: When does === (identity comparison) make sense, versus == (equality)?

options.text: === tests whether two references point to the same instance and is meaningful only for reference types (classes); value types have no meaningful identity — only equality (==) applies, because 'the same value' and 'an equal value' are indistinguishable for them.
=== works on all types and is always preferable to == because it is faster.
=== compares values field-by-field, while == compares memory addresses.
=== is only for strings, == is for everything else.

questions.explanation: The chapter: reference types have identity (=== tests same-instance); value types have only equality since equal and same are indistinguishable. Reaching for === signals genuine reasoning about shared instances. (b) is false — === applies only to reference types and isn't a general speed win. (c) reverses the two operators. (d) fabricates a string-only rule.

questions.prompt: How do value types support Swift's concurrency and data-race safety story?

options.text: A value copied across a task or actor boundary can't be mutated by two tasks at once, because each holds its own independent copy — there's no shared mutable state to race on, which is why Sendable checking scrutinizes classes far more than structs.
Value types are automatically locked by the runtime whenever accessed concurrently, so they serialize all access.
Value types disable concurrency entirely; only classes can be used across threads.
Value types are irrelevant to concurrency; only actors matter.

questions.explanation: The chapter connects value semantics to concurrency: copies mean no shared mutable state, so no race — hence Sendable scrutinizes classes more than structs. (b) fabricates automatic locking; the safety comes from copying, not locks. (c) is false; value types are exactly what's safe to pass across boundaries. (d) understates it — value semantics are foundational to the data-race-safety model, not irrelevant.

questions.prompt: Per the chapter, when is choosing a class over a struct the right call?

options.text: When you genuinely need identity (multiple references to the same mutable object), need inheritance, must interoperate with an Objective-C API requiring it, or are modeling a truly shared resource (like a database connection) rather than a data record.
Always — classes are the modern default and structs are a legacy feature.
Only when the type has more than five properties, since structs become slow beyond that.
Whenever the type needs methods, since structs can't have behavior.

questions.explanation: The chapter's concrete reasons for a class: identity, inheritance, Obj-C interop, or a genuinely shared resource — otherwise struct is the modern default. (b) inverts the guidance (struct is the default). (c) fabricates a property-count performance rule. (d) is false; structs have methods, so 'needs behavior' is not a reason.
